source_display: The Founder You Are (Not Who You Wish)
evidence: You start a company with the founder that you are, not the founder that you wish you could be.

source_display: The Founder You Are (Not Who You Wish)
evidence: This feels very postmortem and I'll tell you, it's refreshing and you know, it's very self-aware to be able to articulate it this close.

source_display: Timing Double-Bind (Too Early AND Too Slow)
evidence: I think our timing was both too early and we were just not fast enough. We kind of got screwed in two directions.

The median user, the median activated user spends 45 minutes or more dumping basically just dumping data into the system. So I think what we've cracked is the UX of getting folks to share about themselves.
Jonny Coreson (Learning Economy Foundation) - Product Lead
Previous founder of Worthy (skills verification/credentials company). Successfully exited Worthy → Learning Economy Foundation
Matthew Cohen - Cohen Strategy Group, Investor / Strategic Advisor. Well connected to adjacent players.
Robert Bagor (Badger), Micro Credential Multiverse. Product: Skills Nexus AI - Building labor market intelligence platform, targeting 1% of Lightcast pricing
Exit opportunities are not discovered through market forces, but through social graphs. The exit market is fundamentally a relationship market, not a market for products.
Asher will provide introductions to private equity firms (specifically KKR) and other potential buyers for Careerspan

Platforms create dependency not because they provide unique value, but because they create monopoly on relationships. Both founders expressed frustration with LinkedIn, yet both depend on LinkedIn data.

Key vulnerability: heavy dependence on LinkedIn API/data exports. Asher notes LinkedIn could change data access policies at any time. Both Vrijen and Asher agree LinkedIn is challenging to work with (Vrijen: 'bullies')

Vrijen shares a document he created about relationship data capture. Philosophy: Importance of capturing relationship signals to build better relationships. The next layer of professional networking software won't be about expanding networks — it will be about scoring and qualifying the relationships that already exist.
Vrijen shares his growing technical skills (semi-technical, can 'carry his own weight'). Both discuss vibe coding and how recent LLM generations (5.0, 4.5 Opus) made it viable.

Asher's enthusiasm for 'vibe coding' revealed a shift in the founder archetype. The old model was: non-technical founder hires technical team. The new model: non-technical founder uses AI to become just technical enough.
Asher reveals Warmer Jobs is running on the side, requiring only ~1 hour/week. Business is barely breaking even. Built Warmer Jobs' network graph capability in 1-2 weeks.

The most valuable asset in the career tech ecosystem is not technology, features, or data — it's the founder network itself. Vrijen mentions wanting to bring together a group of career tech founders in February.
Careerspan is exploring an exit/acquisition opportunity. Team is tired after building the product. Choice between asset sale, acquihire, or MD role.
Tech asset is strong and worth indexing on. Accompanying the tech: Strong POV around disruptive play in HR. Vrijen seeking Asher's thoughts given Asher's experience with exits.
